own_review.point: bundledSignatures = ['jdk-unsafe'] is unversioned; it resolves from targetCompatibility, which Kafka never sets (it uses options.release = minJavaVersion), so the signature set silently follows whatever JDK runs the build instead of the supported baseline (11). Should pin 'jdk-unsafe-11' or set targetCompatibility; on JDKs unknown to forbidden-apis 3.8 the task degrades/skips.

ignoreFailures = true is hardcoded, so the check can never fail; combined with test.dependsOn('forbiddenApisMain') it adds analysis time to every test run with zero enforcement and no follow-up path. Build convention elsewhere is userIgnoreFailures (line 77) or false (spotbugs, line 803).

forbiddenApisTest is not wired, inconsistent with checkstyleMain+checkstyleTest on the same line; also the plugin auto-attaches its tasks to `check`, making the test.dependsOn wiring partially redundant.

Applied to all subprojects unconditionally: it will scan Scala-compiled classes of :core (the build deliberately excludes core from jacoco 'since its a scala project'; scalac emits default-charset/locale calls causing false positives) and generated code (**/generated/** is excluded from spotless/checkstyle/rat but not here).

Plugin declared in plugins{} without `apply false` (so applied to the root project, which has no java plugin) and then re-applied via `apply plugin:` in subprojects; spotbugs/scoverage/shadow use `apply false` for this pattern.
Diff strips the trailing newline at end of build.gradle — gratuitous noise, should be restored.

points.point: Investigation revealed 4 main-source usages jdk-unsafe may flag (ByteBufferUnmapper sun.misc.Unsafe, LoggingSignalHandler sun.misc.Signal, KerberosError sun.security.krb5, KafkaLog4jAppender System.setProperty)
points.why: Files exist but all use reflective Class.forName / System.setProperty — none are covered by the jdk-unsafe bundle (default locale/charset/timezone APIs) and reflective string refs are invisible to bytecode signature checks

points.why: Correct mechanics and flags the real Scala-bytecode risk

qualitative: v2 and v3 tie as best (net +1) but for different reasons: v2 is the most disciplined — it uniquely caught the missing `apply false` on the plugin declaration and the failOnMissingClasses hardening, with only one fabrication (misplacing where the wiki-link comment sits in the diff); v3 dug deepest into the repo and correctly engaged the Scala :core risk, but fabricated its headline 'investigation' claim that four reflective sun.misc/System.setProperty usages would be flagged by jdk-unsafe, which misunderstands both the signature bundle and bytecode-level checking. v1 is weakest (-2): two confident false claims (':core is a no-op' — backwards, the plugin scans compiled classes; and a nonexistent plugin-version-centralization convention). v4 is clean and accurate but shallow — four points, half affirmation, no finding beyond the obvious. Nobody caught the most substantive issue: the unversioned jdk-unsafe signature binds to an unset targetCompatibility, so the check silently varies with the build JDK instead of the JDK 11 baseline; nobody flagged generated-code exclusions either.
